title: Motown, Black Music & Cultural Memory
summary: Trace Black Detroit through Motown, gospel, jazz, riverfront performance, and the institutions that keep the city's musical memory alive.

stops.title: Motown Museum
stops.description: Open at Hitsville U.S.A., where Black Detroit turned studio craft, neighborhood ambition, and national crossover into one of the city's defining cultural exports.

stops.title: New Bethel Baptist Church
stops.description: Move into gospel power and public voice at the church tied to C. L. Franklin, Aretha Franklin, and the spiritual backbone of Black Detroit musical life.

stops.title: The Blue Bird Inn
stops.description: Read Detroit jazz and neighborhood nightlife at a club remembered for Black performance history far beyond the Motown label alone.

stops.title: Charles H. Wright Museum of African American History
stops.description: Add public history and Black cultural stewardship at one of Detroit's strongest institutions for reading music, movement, and memory together.

stops.title: Aretha Franklin Amphitheatre
stops.description: Close on the riverfront at a civic performance space that keeps Black Detroit sound, celebration, and public memory in circulation.

title: Black Bottom, Paradise Valley & Urban Removal
summary: Follow Black Detroit through Black Bottom and Paradise Valley memory, neighborhood removal, public-housing legacy, and the landscapes that replaced a once-dense Black district.

stops.title: Black Bottom at Lafayette Park
stops.description: Open where Black Bottom once stood, reading urban renewal and neighborhood loss through the modern landscape that replaced a dense Black district.

stops.title: Paradise Valley at Gratiot & Randolph
stops.description: Turn toward Paradise Valley, where nightlife, commerce, and Black public life flourished before freeway building and redevelopment reshaped the district.

stops.title: Brewster-Wheeler Recreation Center
stops.description: Add public-housing legacy and neighborhood continuity at a landmark tied to the Brewster-Douglass homes and the lived geography of Black Detroit.

stops.title: Detroit Historical Museum
stops.description: Step into public interpretation at a museum that helps connect Black Bottom, migration, labor, and Detroit's larger urban story.

stops.title: Black Bottom Street Grid at I-375
stops.description: Close along the I-375 corridor, where freeway construction made Black Bottom's removal impossible to mistake and the erased street grid still shapes Detroit memory.

title: Labor, Industry & Black Detroit
summary: Read Black Detroit through factory geography, labor organizing, industrial art, and the working-city institutions that tied production to public power.

stops.title: Ford Piquette Avenue Plant Museum
stops.description: Open where the early auto industry scaled up, using factory space to frame Detroit as a working city long before its Black labor story reached national visibility.

stops.title: Detroit Industry Murals at the DIA
stops.description: Turn to industrial art and working-class imagery at Rivera's murals, where production, labor, and the human body become part of Detroit's civic self-portrait.

stops.title: UAW Solidarity House
stops.description: Move into organized labor at the headquarters of the UAW, where the route can read Black Detroit through bargaining power, worker identity, and industrial politics.

stops.title: Ford Rouge Factory Tour
stops.description: Extend into the Rouge landscape to connect Detroit's Black labor story to the scale, discipline, and danger of the region's defining industrial geography.

stops.title: Monument to Joe Louis
stops.description: Close downtown with a Black Detroit icon whose public image still carries the city's language of strength, endurance, and working-class self-definition.

title: Black Political Power in Detroit
summary: Trace Black Detroit through the Black press, church leadership, nationalist institution-building, public memory, and the city structures where municipal power is exercised.

stops.title: Michigan Chronicle Building
stops.description: Open with Black Detroit's public voice at the paper that helped shape civic debate, political aspiration, and the city's Black public sphere.

stops.title: Second Baptist Church
stops.description: Move into church leadership, abolition memory, and the long Black institutional tradition that fed Detroit's later civic and political power.

stops.title: Shrine of the Black Madonna
stops.description: Read Black political theology and self-determination at a Detroit institution that tied faith, nationalism, and community power together.

stops.title: Rosa Parks Transit Center
stops.description: Add resistance and public memory in the heart of downtown at a transit hub named for a figure whose Detroit years also mattered to movement history.

stops.title: Coleman A. Young Municipal Center
stops.description: Close at the municipal center that makes Black city power unmistakable, tying neighborhood leadership to the machinery of Detroit government.

title: Neighborhood Memory from Midtown to the West Side
summary: Move from Midtown toward the west side through neighborhood art, Black press memory, public trails, and local institutions that keep Detroit's block-level story in view.

stops.title: Detroit Historical Museum
stops.description: Open in Midtown where the city narrates itself, then push outward toward neighborhood memory that often sits beyond downtown focus.

stops.title: Durfee Innovation Society
stops.description: Move into an old school building reworked for neighborhood-scale creativity, education, and mutual support on Detroit's west side.

stops.title: Lincoln Street Art Park
stops.description: Turn to west-side public art, reuse, and neighborhood-scale experimentation at a site where block life becomes a medium for Detroit memory.

stops.title: Marygrove Conservancy
stops.description: Keep heading west through a campus reimagined for neighborhood learning, cultural life, and long-horizon reinvestment in Detroit place.

stops.title: Joe Louis Greenway West Warren Gateway
stops.description: Close on a public path meant to reconnect neighborhoods, reading Detroit's future through mobility, equity, and the block-level stakes of reinvestment.
